answer:
In the Smṛti corpus, particularly the foundational *Manusmṛti*, the *guru* (teacher) is a pivotal figure in the spiritual and ritual life of the twice-born (*dvija*), ranked hierarchically below the *ācārya* (full Vedic teacher) and *upādhyāya* (subsidiary teacher) but above other relations in reverence.[^1] The *guru* is defined as the Brahmin who performs the rites from conception onward and nourishes the pupil with food (*Mn_2.142*), or one who imparts even a small amount of sacred learning (*Mn_2.149*). The student (*brahmacārin*) must dwell with the *guru*, serve him assiduously, observe strict etiquette (e.g., lower seat, inferior food, rising to greet), control senses, and prioritize studies and beneficial acts (*Mn_2.172–245*). Defiling the *guru*'s bed is a *mahāpātaka* (great sin).[^2] Reverence for the *guru* equates to that for parents, as he grants a second, eternal birth via the Veda (*Mn_2.146–148*); his wife is honored equally (*Mn_2.210–217*).

Other Smṛtis echo this: *Aṅgirasa Smṛti* portrays the *guru* as a chastiser of the self-controlled (*Ang_2,6.6*), whose bed-defilement incurs fall (*Ang_2,7.8*), and elevates him in gift-giving (*Ang_1.501*; *Ang_2,5.8* warns non-preceptors). *Kātyāyana Smṛti* references Guru Bṛhaspati on pleas (*K_170*). *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* notes teacher-student bonds like family (*Brh_1,1.123*). The *guru* embodies transmission of *dharma*, demanding utmost obedience for *tapas* and *Brahma*-attainment.

In Old Javanese ethical texts (Kawi), the *guru* is a debt repaid by teaching others (*Kunjarakarna*: "The debt to your teacher is repaid in your own good students"[^3]; alongside debts to God, world, parents). Devotion to the *guru* qualifies disciples for sacred knowledge (*Gaṇapatitattva*: worthy disciple is "devoted to his guru," senses conquered, righteous[^4]). The corpus lacks exhaustive treatment but aligns *guru* with ethical transmission, echoing Smṛti reverence.

The corpus covers these concepts unevenly (37 works total; no *guru*-specific treatises beyond Manu). Unrelated to property/liberty themes in French texts.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, ch.2, [full chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-c52281). Key: [p-36a740](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-36a740); [p-3faca8](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-3faca8); [p-a03329](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/02/translation/#p-a03329).

[^2]: *Aṅgirasa Smṛti*, ch.1, [full chapter](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-9c80a3). Key: [p-f977f9](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-f977f9); [p-cafcb4](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-cafcb4).

[^3]: *Kuñjarakarṇa Dharmakathana*, ch.1, [p-bcd58e–p-0e1648](/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-bcd58e).

[^4]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, ch.1, [p-33578f–p-e941a7](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-33578f).
